sentences.text: Ervin Greenfeld.
Or in Hebrew, it's Chaim.
E-R-V-I-N G-R-E-E-N-F-E-L-D.
August the 31st in 1927.
I just passed 68.
I was born in old Hungary in a town which is spelled G-Y-O-N-G-Y-O-S.
Pronounced, 'Yengesh.'
The town was-- it is still there.
It's not even called a small town today.
About 30,000, 40,000 people there.
Naturally without Jews.
It was a well to do town-- a lot of wineries.
It was not far from Tokaj, which is a famous place for wine.
This town was larger than Tokaj.
And the whole area is-- was full of-- most of the people in my town whom I knew, including my own family, my ancestors-- they were all in-- they had their own wine yards.
And very-- most of the people were well to do people and were exporting a lot of wine.
Grapes and wine.
In the town-- in Gyongyos--
Yeah.
Well, first of all, I didn't stay in Gyongyos too long.
I was about five and half years old maybe when I left town.
We moved over to Czechoslovakia, which was those days Czechoslovakia.
The talking about my family-- it was a very close knit family, as most of the Jewish families used to be very close.
Especially orthodox families, we were all especially close.
My mother had 10 brothers and sisters.
None of them did survive.
My father had one brother and one sister.
And his old father, who was 84 years old-- a very well learned man, and humble, pious man-- was very much respected in the whole town.
His name was-- everybody called him Rebbe Zelig Greenfeld.
People who come from those towns, they still remember the name Rebbe Zelig.
I was told not too long ago by somebody who was telling me that this old man was beaten up in the ghetto before he was thrown into the-- to the train, which took them.
It wasn't a train.
It was actually a cattle car.
Somebody told me this, and I wish she would never have told me this, because it made be very, very unhappy about it.
The way I understand, at his last few days in the ghetto, he was already not completely with himself.
Mentally, he was already-- like he wasn't well.
And I'm just asking myself and asking how it was possible for any human being to take-- to beat up an old man was who was hardly-- he was hardly even walking.
He was an 84 year old man.
But it was a part of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the facts, of those bitter facts in those years.
My father was a--
Nathan Greenfeld.
Nathan-- he was a very, very-- a very intelligent person.
A very good and honest businessman.
A wonderful mouthpiece he had.
Wonderful, beautiful handwriting.
The man probably didn't go more than four elementary schools, and I have letters from him with his signature.
I don't see handwriting like this anymore.
Almost-- it's unbelievable.
There was a case were he was-- for an example, he was accused of something, which it wasn't true.
And he defended himself.
And the judge asked him, Mr.
Greenfeld, which law school did you go to?
I said, I didn't go to any law school.
I just tell you with my own words the way I feel about these accusations.
He was very strong in character.
Couldn't stand anything which wasn't true to the 100%.
And-- and he was a good businessman.
He provided his family.

And even in the hardest times where Jewish children were not welcome in school-- I'm talking about the public school, or high school, or gymnasium-- all of-- all of his children did finished high school and even higher than that.
And we were strictly orthodox people, but education was on top of the list.
Even those very, very hard days I remembered that we were not welcome in the school, and he did not bargain on that.
All of us did finish high school-- 1942, '43, and even 1944.
And this was already days where the anti-Semitism was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all over.
Everybody has effected, and it was in the school itself we were struggling, and felt very uncomfortable, and unwelcome.
I know it for a fact, that in my town for example-- it was a small town that I lived before the war.
The government made collections for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for winter clothing, which was supposed to send out for the American-- for the-- for the Hungarian army.
Naturally, in this small town, they did not have print shops.
So they came to the high school.
Will you please provide us with some beautiful signs.
Those few students who know how to make a nice sign-- have you going to put it out near the public places of the city.
In the movie house, in the big-- in post office.
Wherever there was some-- naturally I was number one who was asked to do it, because I knew how to draw.
And as soon as I was finished with my beautiful sign, I took it in.
Next day, it was out in the biggest place but my name was covered up.
In other words, the Jewish name was not supposed to be recognized anymore.
These were the times.
But my father-- what more can I tell you about my father?
Tell me about his business a little bit.
He was in a business which was actually in Czechoslovakia.
My mother's parents owned a large business in Czechoslovakia in a town which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
They were probably the second largest in big junkyard, textile, and metal scrap iron.
And we were a part of that business.
All of his children were involved in the business, and it was a very successful business.
Whole train loads were coming into our yards and picking up and taking it.
It was export, import, whatever it was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was a-- I wanted to say something about him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was a very, very, very correct person.
And wouldn't-- and anything which had to do with something to do with not the correct way, he was not a part of it.
My mother was like an angel.
Gisella.
It's hard for me even to recognize-- to record-- how-- to picture the way she looked to me.
It was 50 years that I saw her.
I was about 16 and 1/2.
And we were parted like in a second's notice.
She was going-- she was remaining and I was taken into a forced labor camp.
I had three beautiful sisters, and we were three brothers.
Out of the three brothers, my brother and myself-- we did survive.
Yes, I wanted to say about my father is this.
That while he was in a labor camp, and he saw the situation.
He was a very intelligent person, and he saw that the situation was in the late months of 1944, that the Hungary-- Hungary was almost half the occupied by the Russians.
And the Jewish laborers, we were driven and chased toward west.
And he said to himself, no more.
I wouldn't go further, because I know there's no way of surviving it.
If I-- and he was shooting off to hide, remain.
And with a few more colleagues of his, they were waiting until the whole group was marched away.
And they were hiding under a-- under a-- in a hiding place.
And the next day, they were catching a Hungarian truck full of Hungarian soldiers.
And they probably knew that they are Jews.
Still though, my father ran over to them.
And he says, listen, boys.
You have a father and you have a mother, don't you?
And you know there is a god above us.
You must do with this for us.
We need, from you-- you should take us to a town, which is about 50, 60 kilometers.
One of these people in the group was a big owner of forest.
He had his own forest on the border between Hungary and Slovakia.
Do you remember his name?
Just--
His name?
Doctor-- he was a-- Koller [NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
He was a high military officer in the First World War.
A very strong, a very hard man, and he knew what he wanted.
He says, boys, I'll take care of it.
But my father was the one who had the mouth piece to convince these Hungarian soldiers.
Listen, you know there is a god above us.
You must do this to him.
You must do this to us.
And they were cooperating.
And they took him into a town, which is called Rozno, which is a border town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And naturally, they were paid.
And they told him, we know that you are Jews.
But the way the men spoke to us, we just couldn't say no.
This is my father's style in talking.
And they were marching into a forest of this of Mr.
Koller.
Koller.
Koller.
K-O-L-L-E-R.
He died after the war.
He was a very ambitious, big businessmen.
And he was an army officer in the First World War.
They ran into the forest, and they stayed in the forest hiding in the Slovakian forest in the-- I wouldn't say the name, because I'm not so sure how it was spelled.
I think not far from the Carpathian for three months.
December, January, and February.
They were staying in the cold, cold in the woods up high mountains.
And they were many times in big danger because they heard the Germans with dogs looking for partisans in the Slovakian forest.
This is a different story, what happened in Slovakia.
And thank goodness they did survive.
They had to go out every second.
Third day, they went out to a little farm, picked up this some food, bought something.
They had-- so a chicken they picked or somebody-- somebody, one of them picked it up.
And they lived it through.
It was not easy, because it was cold.
And by the time when he came home, his eyes were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] affected from the-- there was a lot of smoke.
They couldn't make a big fire because it was dangerous.
However, there was a smokey little fire to keep them warm enough.
But this is just a little character of his, because he was-- he knew-- he knew what he wanted to do.
He knew what he wanted to say, and he had a very strong character, and a beautiful way of expressing himself.
Probably this was helping him in this situation.
In my town, we had about 170, 180 Jewish families.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was still a young boy, and it was beautiful.
We had much less then what today's children have or demand, but people were happy.
The community life was happy because everybody knew everybody.
I still believe in that in a small town you have more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] closeness to people than in a big town.
As far as the-- well, naturally, we heard already the 19-- early '30s, we heard already what's going on in Germany.
And many, many times, non-Jewish boys used to warn us.
They'd say, hey, listen.
Hitler is coming.
Hitler is coming.
Unfortunately, we didn't realize that this is not-- it wasn't just an empty threat.
Not only we as children-- we took it-- we didn't-- it didn't even bother us.
It was such a natural thing that the Jewish boy went out on the street-- he could wind up with a stone thrown into his head.
That was a natural thing.
I went to a school, high school, with Gentile boys.
And we were on a good term, that-- but there was always-- we had-- they always-- we had to-- they made us feel always that we are Jewish.
And most of the best students were Jewish boys and girls.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it wasn't a long time.
Unfortunately, it wasn't-- so the time period, what I would call my childhood, was a short one.
In the school itself, personally, I did not have.
Naturally, we had such a thing, which is probably going to be very unusual that-- to hear about it.
Every-- once a week, we had to go to which was called like a training, army training.
But the Jewish boys from since 1940 could not participate in that.
We had to go out and do labor.
So there was already a distinction or a separation between the Jewish students and the non-Jewish students.
We had to wear a little yellow band.
And instead of learning about how to use a rifle or anything which had to do with the active military, the Jewish boys didn't have to participate in that, or they were not allowed to participate.
But the whole thing looked funny, because most of the questions what the army officers used to ask-- they used to ask questions.
And in most cases, the Jewish boys knew how to answer.
I remember that there once-- once the army officer, he says, do you know where we are up to?
Where is it?
[NON-ENGLISH].
Where are the Germans?
Where are we now?
And the Jewish boys did read the newspapers, did hear the radio, did know much more about it in comparison to the-- to the other students.
But this is a side issue.
Tell me, in your town, did they have a Jewish newspaper?
Was there a press?
No.
No.
There was no Jewish newspaper.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- somebody was-- I just heard this after the war that many, many years before I could remember they used to have a-- a-- the only thing which we had, it was a-- every Friday, the congregation gave out a newsletter giving us information about the services, what is happening around the synagogue, and so on.
Because Jewish life was concentrated around the synagogue.
However, even though it wasn't a what you call today a very strictly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hasidic town.
It was not.
There was seven, eight doctors.
And most of the people didn't even wear a beard.
Most of the Jews of the town were intelligent, educated people.
The rabbi of the town was an 84-year-old man.
His name was Rebbe Balauity, B-A-L-A-U-I-T-Y.
Balauity.
He was also-- he had probably the same-- the same story as with my grandfather.
He was also taken away and sent to Auschwitz.
It was-- they had young groups, boys.
They had young groups for girls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is besides the synagogue.
We had a Jewish school in town.
Besides the Hebrew school, we had the-- the secular school was also under Jewish auspices.
It was the teacher was a Jewish lady, Ms.
Brown.
I even have one of her report cards which survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had youth groups, I remember.
They had even Zionist groups.
They had a group, which is called the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I used to be invited to sing for them.
I don't know why, but they called on me to sing.
And they had-- around the synagogue, it wasn't active social life what you have here.
For example, a European synagogue did not have the kitchen with a gas range, a Frigidaire.
The place was not for making parties.
What I do remember is that anything like a Purim or a Simchat Torah which was celebrated by the community-- it was in the private homes of better or the well to do people.
And that goes-- even my father had in our own home.
I remember it was a party.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] I also remember my own bar mitzvah, which was held in my own-- in my own-- in my our-- in my home.
Because there was no such thing like a catered affair.
Everything was done homemade.
Unfortunately, by the time I became bar mitzvah, I prepared a beautiful speech.
I still remember.
It was in the late summer in August.
I remember even the parashah of the-- of the-- of the Sidra.
It was Shoftim.
Unfortunately, by the time my turn came, half of the town's Jewish men were already called in for forced labor, because this was in 1940.
I was born in '27, and 13 years later came 1940.
And just a few weeks before my bar mitzvah celebration was supposed to come up, half of the schul was empty.
So I still had my drosha here.
My speech is still here with me, which I only said it to the close family.
It was a very-- it was-- in Yiddish they say it, [YIDDISH].
It was-- it turned out-- turned out a sour one because many people were called away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we also were very, very-- my sister and brothers were very active.
And my father was a very popular man.
And I will never forget that, even though today, it's not customary.
But in our town, it came Hanukkah time, each of the children had his own group.
Two have a little fun, or play around, or play a little cards.
And our-- our family was a special one, because in every room-- we had about seven rooms.
In each room, there was my sister's friends, and my other sister's friends, and my father's friends, and the boys' friends.
We had groups of people.
And everybody had beautiful fun, what you call.
And it wasn't a-- playing cards for the money.
It was just the plain-- very nice social gathering.
That remains with me for-- for as long as I live.
It was beautiful, because my father was a wonderful sense of humor.
And he was going from room to room, and the youngsters were-- they were just-- they loved to hear his remarks and stories.
The name of the town was Tornala.
It's a town today in Slovakia in the southern part of Slovakia.
It's called Tornala-- T-O-R-N-A-L-A.
About my drawing.
Well, I was a-- I knew how to draw.
As a matter of fact, all my sisters, they all-- they were all doing beautiful, beautiful handwriting and very nice drawing.
Singing was a hobby of mine since I was born.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I still do it if I have a chance or if they call up on me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was maybe 13 years old when the cantor of the town, who had a beautiful, beautiful voice-- and he said it to me those days.
I was helping him in his services.
And as a reward, it came Simchat Torah, they usually-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he bought-- he bought a-- the honor to for me that I should perform in the big synagogue.
Which was unheard of at a 13-year-old boy, but I was doing a beautiful job that time.
I made one little mistake, and I was doing a beautiful Simchat Torah services.
I was-- this was-- I was 13 years old and one month old when this happened.
So we had a beautiful choir in the school.
Even in the high school.
And I was always on the-- as a soloist, I was doing singing in the high school.
OK.
We're going to pause in a moment, but I'd like to know, do you remember the cantor's name?
Sure, I remember.
His name was Rhinehertz.
Rebbe Duvid Rhinehertz.
Beautiful, handsome man who was about 6', who knows how long.
And a gorgeous long beard.
And he had a nice family.
His son was a close friend of mine.
And once he said, even I wouldn't-- that he would like me to be his son-in-law.
He had beautiful daughters.
One of-- and we are very-- we kept very close, the families.
But that was-- this is a story when I was 13, 14 years old.
OK.
